Appendix

A Construction of High-Frequency Measures for U.S. Equities

We construct the TAQ effective spreads using theHolden and Jacobsen (2014)methodology and their SAS code. The first step involves constructing and cleaning a complete, official record of NBBO quotes. For the MTAQ period (monthly files), this includes (1) adjusting for withdrawn quotes; (2) using the “interpolated time” technique; (3) deleting periods where the NBBO appears to be locked or crossed; and (4) deleting quotes with abnormal modes, one-sided quotes (either bid or ask prices equal to zero), and quotes with abnormally high bid-ask spreads (greater than $5). For the DTAQ period this involves (1) combining data from both the NBBO file and quote file to construct the official and complete NBBO, and (2) deleting quotes with abnormal modes, crossed quotes (bid > ask), one-sided quotes (either bid or ask prices equal to zero), withdrawn quotes, quotes with abnormally high bid-ask spreads (greater than $5), and quotes where the bid (ask) price is less (greater) than the previous midpoint by $2.5.

The complete and clean NBBO quotes are then merged with trades. During the MTAQ period, trades are time-stamped to the second and we match each trade with the NBBO quote from the previous second. During the DTAQ period, trade and quotes were initially timestamped to the millisecond; on July 25, 2015, the Daily TAQ timestamps were changed to the microsecond; and on October 24, 2016, the timestamps were changed to the nanosecond. During the DTAQ period, we therefore match transactions with the NBBO quotes from the previous millisecond, microsecond, and nanosecond for the three subperiods, respectively. For both periods, we discard trades outside the main trading hours 9:30 a.m.–4:00 p.m. and trades with a proportional effective spread larger than 20%.

FollowingBarndorff-Nielsen et al. (2009)andBollerslev et al. (2018), we apply additional filters to clean the NBBO mid-quotes before calculating the daily realized volatilities. Specifically, we remove (1) quotes where bid-ask spreads larger than 50 times the median bid-ask spread for a given stock and day and (2) quotes where the absolute difference between the mid-quote and the median daily mid-quote exceeds 10 times the average value of this difference on a given day. These filters are designed to remove obvious outliers, such as spurious jumps of the bid and ask prices to $10,000, for example.

How do our TAQ effective spreads compare with the previous literature?Table A3reports the mean and median for our TAQ effective spreads (winsorization at 99%). For the MTAQ period (1994–2003), the mean equals 0.72% (0.71%) and the median equals 0.56% (0.59%) for the monthly (annual) TAQ spread. For the DTAQ period (2003–2019), the mean equals 0.24% (both monthly and annual), and the median equals 0.14% for monthly spreads and 0.15% for annual spreads.

Corwin and Schultz (2012)andAbdi and Ranaldo (2017)consider the full population of U.S. stocks and obtain higher average and median TAQ spreads than we do. Specifically, for the 1993–2006 period, CS report a mean of 2.38% and a median of 1.29%, while AR report, for a similar period (1993–2003), a mean of 2.14% and a median of 1.27%. AR also consider the 2003–2015 period and report a mean of 0.82% and a median of 0.27%. It appears that the difference between our results and those of CS and AR is driven by the very small stocks included in the CS and AR samples – that is, the 2% of stocks by market capitalization not included in the Russell 3000 index. These very small stocks likely trade with relative high effective spreads. This is consistent with the large difference between the mean and median reported by AR and CS which indicates that the distribution of the spreads has a fat right tail. In contrast, we observe much smaller differences between the means and medians in our sample, suggesting that our results are less driven by stocks in the right tail of the distribution.

Others in the literature consider a randomly selected subset of stocks.Goyenko, Holden, and Trzcinka (2009)andHolden (2009)consider 400 NYSE, AMEX, and NASDAQ stocks for the 1993–2005 period, whileHasbrouck (2009)sample includes 300 NYSE and NASDAQ stocks stratified by market capitalization for the 1993–2006 period.Goyenko, Holden, and Trzcinka (2009)report a mean of 2.9% (2.6%) and a median of 1.6% (1.6%) for monthly (annual) TAQ spreads. These results are similar to those for CS and AR. However,Hasbrouck (2009)reports a mean of 1.06% and a median of 0.54% for annual spreads. These values are quite close to our annual MTAQ values. Thus, the choice of the sample of stocks can have a sizable impact on the average spreads.

B Construction of Low-Frequency Measures for U.S. Equities

Following the previous literature, we apply several minimum data requirements and data adjustments when construction the low-frequency measures. Note that since we only include a stock-month in our analysis if none of the low-frequency measures returns a missing value, the minimum data requirements for an individual measure effectively apply to all measures. Unless stated otherwise, the measures are implemented in the Ox language of Doornik (2018), and the code is available on request.

R: No individual data minimum data requirements or price adjustments.H: FollowingHasbrouck (2009), we setαβα=β=1e−12andσσs2=0.052⁠, but also consider a tighter prior onswithσσs2=0.012⁠.CS: FollowingCorwin and Schultz (2012), we (1) keep the previous daily high and low prices on those days when a stock does not trade, or has a zero price range, and, on days with a zero range, we adjust the high and low prices as explained in section II.B. of their paper; (2) adjust for nonzero overnight returns as explained in section II.A. of the paper; and (3) discard stock-months with fewer than 12 two-day estimates. We use the SAS code kindly provided by CS on their website to calculate the monthly measures and augment the code to calculate the annual measures (the only difference being that we average daily observations over a year rather than a month).AR: FollowingAbdi and Ranaldo (2017), we (1) discard stock-months with less than 12 applicable days, where an applicable day is defined as a day with positive high, low, and closing prices CRSP, and a positive range (high minus low) and (2) keep the previous daily high, low, and close prices on those days when a stock does not trade, or has a zero price range. We use the SAS code kindly provided by AR on their website to calculate the monthly measures and augment the code to calculate the annual measures (the only difference being that we average daily observations over a year rather than a month).EFT: Similar toHolden (2009), we use the fractional grid with a minimum increment of164during the period prior to decimalization in April 2001 and a decimal grid thereafter.FHT: FollowingFong, Holden, and Trzcinka (2017), we require at least 11 nonzero daily returns in a month. Otherwise, we set the value of FHT as missing.BA: FollowingAbdi and Ranaldo (2017), we discard daily bid-ask spreads if they are nonpositive or greater than 50% before calculating monthly or annual averages. If no valid daily bid-ask spreads are observed for a given month, we consider the BA for that month missing.

Table A3report summary statistics for these measures. We find that the measures are, on average, smaller than those reported by the previous studies mentioned inAppendix A. This is not surprising because the TAQ spreads are also smaller in our sample, on average. As discussed before, this difference is likely driven by the exclusion from our sample of the very small stocks that are not members of the Russell 3000 index. These very small stocks compose only around 2% of U.S. stock market capitalization. But because they are numerous and tend to have relatively large spreads, they have an outsized influence on the sample average.
